cards.back: Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor. The name describes its physical structure (metal gate, oxide insulator, semiconductor substrate) and operating principle (electric field controls current).

cards.front: Name the four terminals of a MOSFET.
cards.back: Gate (G), Drain (D), Source (S), and Body/Bulk (B). The body terminal connects to the semiconductor substrate. In many circuits, the body is tied to the source.

cards.front: What is the role of the SiO₂ oxide layer?
cards.back: It insulates the gate from the substrate, ensuring zero DC gate current (iG = 0). The gate controls the channel entirely through an electric field penetrating the oxide — no current needs to flow.

cards.front: Why is the gate current iG = 0 in a MOSFET?
cards.back: Because the gate is completely insulated from the substrate by the SiO₂ oxide layer. This is a near-perfect insulator, so no DC current can flow into the gate. This gives the MOSFET essentially infinite input impedance.

cards.front: What does the W/L (aspect) ratio control?
cards.back: The current-carrying capability of the MOSFET. IDS ∝ W/L. Doubling the width or halving the length doubles the drain current for the same bias conditions.

cards.front: What is the 'channel' in a MOSFET?
cards.back: A thin layer of charge carriers (inversion layer) that forms at the semiconductor surface between source and drain when sufficient gate voltage is applied. This creates a conductive path for current flow.

cards.front: How does MOSFET control differ from BJT control?
cards.back: MOSFET: voltage-controlled (VGS controls IDS, iG = 0). BJT: current-controlled (IB controls IC, IB > 0). The MOSFET has much higher input impedance because no input current is needed.

cards.front: What passive component is the MOS structure analogous to?
cards.back: A parallel-plate capacitor. The metal gate is one plate, the substrate is the other, and the oxide is the dielectric. Cox = εox/tox × W × L.

cards.front: In an NMOS device, what type are the source/drain regions and substrate?
cards.back: Source and drain: n⁺ (heavily doped n-type). Substrate: p-type. The channel that forms is n-type (electron conduction). The 'N' in NMOS refers to the channel carrier type.

cards.front: What is the typical gate oxide thickness in modern MOSFETs?
cards.back: 1–10 nm (nanometres) — just a few atoms thick. Thinner oxide gives stronger gate control over the channel but is harder to manufacture without defects.

cards.front: Why do MOSFETs dominate digital logic instead of BJTs?
cards.back: Three key advantages: (1) Zero gate current → low power at scale, (2) Small physical size → billions fit on one chip, (3) Simple fabrication → easy to manufacture. The zero gate current is most critical — it means controlling each transistor costs essentially no power.

cards.front: What is channel length L vs channel width W?
cards.back: L = distance between source and drain (direction of current flow). W = lateral extent of the channel (perpendicular to current flow). L determines resistance; W determines how much current can flow.
